title: Board and Batten Bunk System — Top Bunk
summary: The top-bunk module of a cabin-style board-and-batten bunk system: board-and-batten headboard panels, 2x2 legs, 1x6 side rails, and a bolted-on guardrail.
description:
This is the top-bunk half of a modular cabin bunk system, built in a cozy board-and-batten cottage style. It's a framed panel headboard/footboard on 2x2 legs, joined to 1x6 side rails, with a slatted mattress support and — critically for a top bunk — a bolted-on guardrail. It's a moderate build: pocket-hole joinery throughout, all straight cuts, but with several fit and safety details that matter because a child sleeps up high on it.

The panels are the board-and-batten look: a plywood (or MDF/particle board) panel topped with a 2x2 and trimmed at the bottom with a 1x6, with 1x2 battens applied to the face. Build them first and drill the pocket holes you'll need to attach the legs before assembly, since it's much easier before the panel is in the frame.

Two safety-driven decisions. First, the side rails: pocket holes are fine for a child, but the plan explicitly says to use bed-rail brackets instead if an adult or large teen will use the top bunk — the extra load warrants it. Second, the guardrail is bolted (with nuts and washers), not screwed, and placed symmetrically — a guardrail is the one part on a top bunk that absolutely cannot work loose, so mechanical fasteners are the right call. The mattress rides on removable 1x3 slats (screwed, not glued, so they lift out) or a bunkie board. Add 1x2 headboard battens for the decorative board-and-batten finish.

tools.note: Panels, legs, side rails, and guardrail all use pocket holes.

materials.name: 1x6, 10 ft
materials.note: Side rails and panel bottom trim.

materials.name: Bunkie board or slat lumber
materials.note: Mattress support to spec — not included in the base list.

materials.name: Wood screws, 1-1/4 in
materials.note: Slats — no glue, so they stay removable.

materials.name: Guardrail bolts, nuts, washers
materials.note: Bolted, not screwed — a guardrail must not work loose.

materials.name: Bed-rail brackets
materials.note: Recommended if an adult/teen uses the top bunk.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut two panels at 39" from the 3/4 in plywood. Cut two panel tops at 39", four legs at 24" and two cleats at 75" from the 2x2. Cut two panel bottom trim at 39" and two side rails at 75" from the 1x6. Cut 28 guardrail rails at 8", four guardrail ends at 16" and ten headboard trim battens at 16-1/4" from the 1x2. Cut one guardrail top (ladder side) at 63-1/2", one guardrail top (back side) at 75", one guardrail bottom (ladder side) at 60-1/2" and one guardrail bottom (back side) at 72" from the 1x3. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to five 8 ft 2x2, two 10 ft 1x6, six 8 ft 1x2 and four 8 ft 1x3. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw — several parts here are within half an inch of each other and are easy to mix up later.

steps.title: Build the board-and-batten panels
steps.body: Build the two panels — plywood/MDF/particle board with the 2x2 top and 1x6 bottom trim. Drill the pocket holes you'll need for attaching the legs in the next step now, while the panels are still easy to work.

steps.title: Add the legs
steps.body: Attach the legs to the panels with pocket holes set for 3/4" stock and glue, using 1-1/4" pocket screws. Also attach to the header — a pocket-hole set for 1-1/2" stock with 2-1/2" screws is stronger there, though a 2" nail works if your panel pocket hole is at the top.

steps.title: Attach the side rails
steps.body: Drill the side-rail pocket holes set for 3/4" stock and attach the 1x6 rails to the leg assemblies. Important: if an adult or a large child/teen will use the top bunk, use bed-rail brackets here instead of pocket holes — the extra load warrants the stronger connection.

steps.title: Build and bolt on the guardrail
steps.body: The guardrail is optional but strongly advised on a top bunk. Drill one pocket hole on the inside of each rail and one on the top of each end, plus two on the ends of the bottom boards, and build the guardrails. Attach them directly to the side rail with bolts, nuts, and washers — not screws — placing the bolts symmetrically. A guardrail is the one part that must never work loose, which is why it's bolted.

steps.title: Add the mattress support
steps.body: Attach the 2x2 cleats to the inside of the side rails, then either lay a bunkie board across them or add 1x3 slats spaced to your mattress recommendations. Screw the slats down with 1-1/4" screws and NO glue, so they stay removable for cleaning and mattress changes.

steps.title: Add the batten trim and finish
steps.body: For the decorative board-and-batten look, add the 1x2 headboard battens across the panels with glue and 1-1/4" finish nails, evenly spaced. Fill holes, sand, and finish or paint.

images.alt: Board-and-batten style top bunk with a guardrail
